title: Microwave-to-Optical Quantum Transduction via Defect-Mediated Scattering in Diamond
authors: Kyosuke Goto, Hodaka Kurokawa, Hideo Kosaka, Kazuki Koshino
abstract: Scaling up superconducting quantum processors remains a central challenge for realizing fault-tolerant quantum computation. Although distributed architectures based on optical photons offer a promising route to scalability, they require an efficient microwave-to-optical quantum transducer that operates at cryogenic temperatures. Existing approaches typically rely on strong optical pumping, which induces undesirable heating and degrades single-photon coherence. Here, we propose a microwave-to-optical quantum transducer based on double-resonant scattering from a single color center embedded in a diamond optomechanical resonator. We show that strong coupling between the color center and the optical cavity enables coherent conversion at extremely low pump powers on the order of 10 pW. The proposed device enables remote entanglement generation on the order of 1 kHz with a fidelity exceeding 0.9, demonstrating a viable pathway toward ultra-low-power, high-efficiency quantum transducers based on individual solid-state defects for future distributed superconducting quantum networks.
summary: This paper proposes a novel quantum transducer using a single color center in a diamond resonator to convert microwave signals to optical photons. By operating under strong coupling and at ultra-low pump powers, the device achieves high efficiency and enables remote entanglement generation. This work presents a promising, low-power solid-state route for building scalable quantum networks.
main_problem: The central challenge is developing an efficient, low-power microwave-to-optical quantum transducer for scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computation at cryogenic temperatures.
main_result: The proposed device demonstrates a viable pathway by achieving a coherent conversion efficiency of 0.32 at extremely low pump powers (55 pW), enabling remote entanglement generation at $\sim 1$ kHz with fidelity exceeding 0.9.
method: The analysis uses theoretical modeling involving the steady-state approximation for coupled cavity modes and calculating the single-photon response via Taylor series expansion of the output photon flux.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum transducer based on a single color center ($	ext{NV}^0$) embedded in a diamond optomechanical resonator, modeled by coupling three resonators: microwave, mechanical, and optical.
key_observables: Coherent conversion efficiency ($P_{coh}$), remote entanglement generation rate ($R_{herald}$), and entanglement fidelity ($F_{1c}$).
important_parameters: Operating at the triply resonant condition ($\omega_a = \omega_b = \omega_{10}$); low pump power ($\sim 55 	ext{ pW}$); and the comparison between spectral diffusion and conversion timescales ($	au_{diff}$ vs $	au_{conv}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the drive frequency component is separable from the converted frequency component by a filter, and the single-photon response is obtained by linearizing the output flux calculation around zero input power.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mode coupling diagram and the schematic of the transduction process; Figure 2 shows the dependence of total and coherent photon flux on input power, and the impact of spectral diffusion rates on efficiency.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model and Hamiltonian, then derives the input-output relation, calculates the single-photon response by linearizing the output flux, and finally discusses the performance metrics like entanglement generation rate and fidelity, while addressing noise limitations from spectral diffusion.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses a critical hardware bottleneck—the quantum interface—for distributed quantum networks, providing concrete metrics (efficiency, rate, fidelity) for a solid-state platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Mode coupling diagram. We operate in the triply resonant condition where the microwave mode, the mechanical mode, and the |0⟩↔|1⟩transition satisfy 𝜔𝑎= 𝜔𝑏= 𝜔10. The optical mode is tuned to the |0⟩↔|2⟩transition. (b) Schematic illustration of the transduction process in the reduced model. The microwave and optical waveguides couple directly to an NV0 center. A pump tone drives the |1⟩↔|2⟩transition resonantly at 𝜔21 = 𝜔20 −𝜔10.

FIG. 2. Single-photon conversion process. (a) Total detected photon flux 𝑅tot (blue) and coherent photon flux 𝑅coh (red) as functions of incident signal microwave photon flux |𝐸s|2. The dashed lines indicate the tangents at the origin; their slopes give the corresponding conversion efficiencies for a single incident microwave photon, 𝑃tot and 𝑃coh, obtained by linear regression. The inset shows 𝑃coh as a function of the signal-frequency detuning Δ𝜇s. The conversion bandwidth is determined from the full width at half maximum of the curve shown in the inset. (b) Coherent conversion efficiency 𝑃coh (red) and dark count rate (purple) versus pump power. The vertical dotted line indicates 55 pW....

FIG. 4. Comparison of conversion efficiency versus pump power with the literature. References: Zhao, 2025 [13]; Weaver, 2024 [11]; Jiang, 2023 [9]; Warner, 2025 [7]; Xie, 2025 [20]; Xu, 2021 [5]; Gard, 2017 [32]; Sahu, 2022 [6].

FIG. 3. Remote entanglement generation. (a) Microwave photons entangled with superconducting qubits (SC qubits) are converted into optical photons by quantum transducers (QTs) and injected into an interferometer, generating remote entanglement. (b) Fidelity and (c) heralding rate as functions of the pure-dephasing rates 𝛾𝜙 1 and 𝛾𝜙 2 for 𝜏diff < 𝜏conv. The red dashed line indicates the contour 𝑃(A) coh /𝑃(A) tot = 0.5. (d) Fidelity and (e) heralding rate as functions of the detunings Δ𝜔10 and Δ𝜔20 for 𝜏diff > 𝜏conv.
